notes: King of Osroene (c. 177–212) who converted to Christianity c. 200, making Edessa likely the first Christian state; distinct from the legendary Abgar V of the Eusebius HE 1.13 correspondence tradition.
Traditional evangelist of Edessa sent by the apostle Thomas according to Eusebius HE 1.13; identified with Thaddaeus in some traditions; subject of the later Doctrine of Addai (c. 390–400).
Paralyzed man in Lydda healed through Peter's ministry in Acts 9.
Prophet who comes down from Judea and warns Paul in Acts 21.
Client king who comes to Caesarea and hears Paul's case in Acts 25.

Proto-martyr of Britain; Roman soldier or citizen who sheltered a Christian priest and was executed; traditionally the first British martyr; date debated between Severan (c. 209) and Diocletianic (c. 304) persecution.
Figure put forward in the Ephesian uproar who attempts to address the crowd in Acts 19.

Bishop of Aelia Capitolina (Jerusalem) c. 212–251; invited Origen to preach in Caesarea c. 215; co-founded the theological library at Caesarea with Origen; imprisoned and died during the Decian persecution.
Wealthy Alexandrian convert from Valentinianism; became Origen's patron and literary sponsor, funding scribes and secretaries for his massive literary output; deacon of the church.
High priest who comes down to Caesarea to accuse Paul in Acts 24.
Disciple in Damascus who restores Saul's sight and baptizes him in Acts 9.
High priest who convenes the Sanhedrin that condemns James in Josephus Antiquities 20.200.
Brother of Simon Peter.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 as Paul's kinsman and fellow prisoner.
Faithful witness named among the believers in Pergamum in Revelation 2.

Syriac church father in the Persian Empire; wrote 23 Demonstrations (337–345) on Christian faith and practice; possibly a bishop; used the Diatessaron as his gospel text; his theology is notably independent of Greek influence.
Alexandrian Christian teacher active at Ephesus and in Achaia.
Believer greeted alongside Philemon and Archippus in the Epistle to Philemon.
Jewish Christian coworker of Paul associated with Corinth and Ephesus.
Believer greeted in Philemon and mentioned in connection with Colossae.
Thessalonian companion of Paul named among the Acts 20 travel party.
Athenian Christian philosopher who wrote an early apology addressed to the emperor Hadrian or Antoninus Pius c. 125-130.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 among the Rome-network households.

Bishop (Patriarch) of Alexandria c. 328–373; chief defender of Nicene Trinitarian orthodoxy against Arianism; exiled five times; consecrated Frumentius as bishop of Aksum; author of Life of Antony, On the Incarnation, and Festal Letters.
Athenian Christian philosopher who addressed an embassy to Marcus Aurelius and Commodus c. 177 defending Christians and articulating Logos theology.

Syriac philosopher-theologian born in Edessa; wrote the Book of Laws of Countries defending free will against fate; initially associated with Valentinian Gnosticism per Eusebius HE 4.30 but later opposed it; founded an influential school at Edessa; refuted by Ephrem.
Early missionary coworker of Paul and prominent figure in Antioch.
Member of the Twelve in the Synoptic apostolic lists.

Gnostic teacher active in Alexandria under Hadrian and Antoninus Pius (c. 117–145). Taught that the supreme God (Abraxas) was unknowable; 365 heavens; the God of the Jews was the lowest angel. Infamously denied that Christ suffered — teaching that Simon of Cyrene was crucified in his place while Jesus stood by and laughed.
Royal figure who comes with Agrippa to Caesarea and hears Paul's case in Acts 25.
Bishop of Bostra in Arabia; held monarchian views denying Christ's pre-existent divinity; corrected by Origen at a synod c. 244; subsequently returned to orthodoxy per Eusebius HE 6.33.
High priest in Jerusalem during the climactic Jerusalem episodes.

Bishop of Rome c. 217–222; former slave who rose to become deacon under Zephyrinus and then pope; extended absolution to Christians who had committed grave sins after penance, provoking the schism of Hippolytus; accused by Hippolytus of Sabellian sympathies.
Associate in Troas with whom Paul left his cloak and books according to 2 Timothy.

North African confessor during the Decian persecution c. 250; endured prolonged imprisonment and torture; his grandmother, uncle, and another uncle had earlier been martyred; Cyprian later appointed him as reader in the Carthaginian church; exemplar of the confessor phenomenon that complicated the lapsed controversy.

Gnostic-adjacent teacher active in Asia Minor, associated with Ephesus c. 90–120. Distinguished between the earthly Jesus (son of Joseph and Mary) and the heavenly Christ (a divine power that descended at baptism and departed before the crucifixion). Opposed the orthodox understanding of a personal incarnation of the Logos.

Head of the Catechetical School of Alexandria; author of Protrepticus, Paedagogus, and Stromateis; synthesized Christian faith with Greek philosophy; key witness for Alexandrian baptismal theology, eucharistic practice, asceticism, and apostolic tradition.
Bishop of Rome associated with the First Epistle of Clement c. 95-96; key early witness to Roman church order and apostolic memory.
Roman emperor 337–361; son of Constantine I; favored Arian/semi-Arian theology; exiled Athanasius; sent Theophilus the Indian on a diplomatic-missionary embassy to Himyar c. 354.
Roman centurion at Caesarea whose household is incorporated into the Jesus movement in Acts 10.
Bishop of Rome c. 251–253; key figure in the Novatian controversy; excommunicated Novatian and his followers; received the support of Cyprian of Carthage.
Pauline associate said in 2 Timothy to have gone to Galatia.
Synagogue ruler in Corinth who believes with his household in Acts 18.

Bishop of Carthage c. 248–258; major Latin theologian of ecclesiology and sacramental theology; converted c. 246; deeply influenced by Tertullian; key figure in the Novatian controversy (supported Cornelius against Novatian); martyred under Valerian.
Athenian woman named among those who believe Paul's message in Acts 17.
Pauline associate said in 2 Timothy to have gone to Thessalonica.
Silversmith in Ephesus who stirs opposition in Acts 19.

Bishop of Alexandria c. 189–232; appointed the young Origen to head the Catechetical School c. 203; later condemned Origen's ordination in Caesarea and expelled him from Alexandria c. 231–232; convened two synods against Origen.
Athenian Areopagite named among those who believe Paul's message in Acts 17.

Student of Origen; bishop of Alexandria c. 248–264; key figure in the Trinitarian controversies after Origen; opposed Sabellianism but was himself accused of tritheism; major voice during the Decian persecution and the Novatian controversy; extensive correspondence preserved by Eusebius.

Bishop of Corinth c. 166–174. Prolific letter-writer whose correspondence with churches in Athens, Sparta, Nicomedia, Gortyna (Crete), Amastris (Pontus), and Rome is recorded by Eusebius (HE 4.23). A key witness to the network of apostolic churches in the 2nd century and the authority of Peter and Paul at Rome and Corinth.
Wife of Felix who hears Paul in Caesarea in Acts 24.
Colossian coworker associated with the churches of Colossae, Laodicea, and Hierapolis.
Philippian envoy and coworker mentioned in Philippians.

Pre-eminent Syriac hymnographer and theologian; deacon at Nisibis under Jacob then relocated to Edessa after 363; wrote Hymns on Faith, Paradise, Nativity, Against Heresies, Nisibis Hymns, and Commentary on the Diatessaron; Doctor of the Church.
Pauline associate said to remain at Corinth in 2 Timothy.
Philippian woman urged to live in harmony in Philippians.

Bishop of Caesarea c. 313–339; author of the Ecclesiastical History, Life of Constantine, and other works; key source for early church history; present at the Council of Nicaea 325; moderate position on Arianism.
King of the Aksumite Empire c. 330–356; converted to Christianity under Frumentius's influence; made Christianity the state religion of Aksum; known from inscriptions and coins.
Bishop of Rome c. 236–250; brought the bodies of Hippolytus and Pontian back from Sardinia; organized Roman clergy into seven diaconal regions; martyred under Decius.
Roman governor who hears Paul's case at Caesarea in Acts 23-24.
Roman governor who succeeds Felix and hears Paul's case at Caesarea in Acts 25.

Bishop of Caesarea in Cappadocia c. 230–268; close ally of Origen and then of Cyprian in the rebaptism controversy; wrote a letter (Ep. 75 in Cyprian's collection) strongly supporting Cyprian's position that heretical baptism is invalid; attended the Synod of Antioch against Paul of Samosata but died en route c. 268.
First bishop of Aksum (Ethiopia); Phoenician from Tyre shipwrecked in Eritrea; consecrated bishop by Athanasius of Alexandria c. 328–333; converted King Ezana; primary witness is Rufinus HE 1.9.
Companion of Paul identified as from Derbe in Acts 20.
Roman proconsul of Achaia during Paul's Corinth hearing in Acts 18.

Student of Origen in Caesarea c. 233–238; bishop of Neocaesarea in Pontus c. 240–270; missionary who reportedly converted most of Pontus; his Address of Thanksgiving to Origen is a key source for Origen's teaching method; credited with many miracles.
Jewish-Christian historian who traveled from Corinth to Rome under Bishop Anicetus; compiled succession lists of Roman bishops; five books of Memoirs (Ὑπομνήματα) preserved in fragments by Eusebius.

Student of Origen and his assistant at the Catechetical School; bishop of Alexandria c. 232–248 after Demetrius; first Alexandrian bishop to use the title 'pope'; continued Origen's theological legacy in Alexandria.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 among the Rome-network households.
Author of the Shepherd of Hermas associated with the Roman church; brother of Pope Pius I according to the Muratorian Fragment.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 among the Rome-network households.
Herodian ruler identified contextually as the Herod who persecutes the Jerusalem church in Acts 12.
Tetrarch of Galilee and Perea in the ministry narratives.

Roman presbyter and theologian; prolific Greek-speaking writer in Rome; opposed the modalism of Zephyrinus and Callistus; set himself up as rival bishop c. 217 (sometimes called the first antipope); exiled with Pontian to Sardinia in 235; reconciled before death; author of the Apostolic Tradition, Refutation of All Heresies, Against Noetus, and Commentary on Daniel.
Bishop of Antioch martyred in Rome, early witness to episcopacy and eucharistic realism.

Bishop of Lyons (Lugdunum) and major anti-gnostic theologian; pupil of Polycarp; author of Against Heresies c. 180, the foundational work on apostolic succession, rule of faith, and refutation of Valentinianism and Marcionism.
First attested bishop of Nisibis; attended the Council of Nicaea (325); mentor of Ephrem the Syrian; credited with building the first church in Nisibis.
One of the first called disciples.
Leading Jerusalem figure in the apostolic period.
Host in Thessalonica whose house is attacked during the mission in Acts 17.
Seed dataset focuses on public-ministry period claims.
Mission helper associated with Barnabas and Paul in Acts 13 and later tradition.
One of the first called disciples.
Prophetic forerunner active in the Judean wilderness.
Council member who requests the body of Jesus for burial.
Householder in Damascus on Straight Street with whom Saul is staying in Acts 9.
Disciple who leads those arresting Jesus in the passion narratives.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 among the Roman saints.
Roman centurion who escorts Paul and shows him kindness at Sidon in Acts 27.

Christian chronographer and polymath; compiled the Chronographiae (five books covering world history to 221); correspondent of Origen on the Susanna question; active in Emmaus-Nicopolis; designed the library at the Pantheon in Rome for Emperor Alexander Severus.
Believer greeted with Andronicus in Romans 16 and said to be in Christ before Paul.
Christian apologist and philosopher martyred in Rome, rich source for mid-2nd-century liturgy.
Brother of Mary and Martha in Bethany.

Bishop of Rome c. 253–254; successor of Cornelius; briefly exiled and returned; continued Cornelius's moderate policy on readmission of the lapsed; Cyprian wrote to congratulate him on his return from exile.
Prophet or teacher from Cyrene named in the church at Antioch in Acts 13.
Pauline companion traditionally associated with Luke and Acts.
Early believer associated with the church in Philippi.
Prophet or teacher at Antioch said to have been brought up with Herod the tetrarch in Acts 13.

Wealthy shipowner from Sinope in Pontus; came to Rome c. 140; rejected the OT and constructed a reduced canon; excommunicated from the Roman church c. 144; major polemical target of Justin, Irenaeus, and Tertullian.
Sister of Mary and Lazarus in Bethany.
Female disciple associated with Galilee, the passion, and the empty tomb narratives.
Mother of Jesus, present in the infancy, passion, and early Jerusalem-community traditions.
Believer in Rome commended for hard labor in Romans 16.
Member of the Twelve in Gospel and Acts lists.

Montanist prophetess and one of the founding triad of the New Prophecy alongside Montanus and Priscilla. Active in Phrygia c. 155–179. Her prophecies were recorded and cited by both Montanist supporters and anti-Montanist critics in Eusebius HE 5.
Bishop of Sardis whose Peri Pascha is the earliest extant Christian homily, presenting Christ as Passover lamb and pre-existent Lord.
Early disciple from Cyprus who hosts Paul's company according to Acts 21.

Founder of Montanism (the 'New Prophecy') in Phrygia c. 155–180. Claimed to speak as the Paraclete in the first person. Associated with prophetesses Maximilla and Priscilla (Prisca). Montanism taught rigorous fasting, the imminent New Jerusalem descending at Pepuza, and the continuation of ecstatic prophecy — generating controversy with emerging episcopal authority.
Disciple from Cana associated with Philip in the Gospel of John.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 with his sister among the Roman saints.
Roman emperor associated with the Great Fire of Rome context and the subsequent persecution of Christians in Tacitus.
Jerusalem Pharisee who appears in the Gospel of John.

Roman presbyter and theologian; author of De Trinitate (c. 250), the first systematic Latin treatise on the Trinity; rigorist who opposed the readmission of the lapsed after the Decian persecution; set up a rival church c. 251 against Pope Cornelius; his followers (Novatianists) persisted for centuries.
Believer in Laodicea associated with a house church in Colossians 4.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 among the Roman saints.
Believer commended to Philemon and linked with Colossae in the Pauline correspondence.

Leading Alexandrian theologian and biblical scholar; student of Clement of Alexandria and eventual head of the Catechetical School. Prolific author covering exegesis, apologetics, and systematic theology. Active in Alexandria (c. 202–232) then Caesarea.

Head of the Catechetical School of Alexandria before Clement; former Stoic philosopher converted to Christianity. According to Eusebius, he traveled as far as India as a missionary. He was Clement of Alexandria's primary teacher.

Bishop of Hierapolis in Asia Minor; wrote the Exposition of Dominical Oracles (5 books) c. 95-110, preserved by Eusebius; heard John the elder directly; key early witness for oral apostolic tradition and millenarianism.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 among the Rome-network households.

name_alt: Saul of Tarsus; Paul the Apostle
notes: Apostolic missionary central to Acts and several New Testament letters.
Beloved believer greeted in Romans 16 as one who labored much in the Lord.
Believer addressed by Paul in the personal letter concerning Onesimus.
Member of the Twelve; distinct from Philip the evangelist.
Missionary figure associated with Samaria and later Caesarea in Acts.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 among the Rome-network households.

Arian church historian; wrote a 12-book Ecclesiastical History (c. 425) covering 300–425; survives mostly in Photius's epitome; key source for Theophilus the Indian's mission and Constantius II's religious diplomacy.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 among the Rome-network households.
Trusted woman commended by Paul in Romans 16.
Bishop of Smyrna martyred c. 155-156; disciple of the apostle John; correspondent of Ignatius and Rome.

Bishop of Ephesus; leader of the Asian Quartodeciman tradition; wrote to Victor of Rome c. 190 defending 14 Nisan observance by appeal to Philip the Apostle, John, Polycarp, and seven of his episcopal ancestors.
Bishop of Rome c. 230–235; exiled with Hippolytus to Sardinia under Maximinus Thrax in 235; resigned the papacy before death; bodies of both were brought back to Rome under Fabian.

Deacon of Cyprian of Carthage; accompanied Cyprian into exile and was present at his martyrdom in 258; author of the Vita Cypriani, the earliest Latin Christian biography; provides key details about Cyprian's conversion, episcopate, and death.
Roman prefect of Judea during the later ministry period.
Coworker of Paul associated with Corinth and Ephesus.
Montanist prophetess and founding triad member alongside Montanus and Maximilla. Active in Phrygia c. 155–179. Her prophecies were recorded and cited by both supporters and anti-Montanist critics.
Leading Maltese host who receives Paul and his companions in Acts 28.

Historian, theologian, translator; continued Eusebius's Church History in two Latin books (HE 10–11, c. 402); primary Western source for Frumentius and the christianization of Aksum; translated many of Origen's works into Latin.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 as chosen in the Lord.

Gnostic teacher in Antioch c. 100–130, contemporary with Basilides. Taught that the world was made by seven angels; the God of the Jews was one of these angels. The Saviour was unborn, incorporeal, and only appeared to be human. Declared marriage and procreation to be inventions of Satan.
Thessalonian companion of Paul named among the Acts 20 travel party.
Roman proconsul at Paphos who is said to believe in Acts 13.
Sasanian emperor (309–379) who persecuted Christians in the Persian Empire beginning c. 340, partly motivated by Constantine declaring Rome a Christian empire; Aphrahat wrote during this persecution.
Missionary coworker associated with Paul and the Jerusalem church.
Prophet or teacher named in the church at Antioch in Acts 13.

Samaritan sorcerer mentioned in Acts 8:9–24; regarded by Irenaeus and Justin as the father of all heresies and root of Gnosticism. He claimed to be the supreme divine Power. His companion Helena he called the first divine Thought (Ennoia). Opposed by Peter at Samaria.
Disciple called during Galilean ministry.
Tanner in Joppa with whom Peter stays in Acts 9-10.
Companion of Paul identified as from Berea in Acts 20.
Figure beaten before Gallio's tribunal in Corinth in Acts 18.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 as beloved by Paul.
Early Jerusalem believer remembered as a martyr in Acts.

Bishop of Rome c. 254–257; central figure in the rebaptism controversy; insisted that heretical baptism was valid if administered in the trinitarian formula, opposing Cyprian and the African councils; invoked the authority of the Roman see and the tradition of Peter's chair; threatened to excommunicate the African and Asian bishops who rebaptized.
Philippian woman urged to live in harmony in Philippians.
Disciple in Joppa known for good works and raised after death in Acts 9.
Student of Justin Martyr in Rome; wrote the Oration to the Greeks c. 155-165 and compiled the Diatessaron (harmony of four Gospels); later became an encratite ascetic and returned to the East.

First major Latin Christian author; active in Carthage c. 195–220. Wrote at least 31 works spanning apologetics, anti-heretical polemic, and moral discipline. Coined 'trinitas' and 'tres personae una substantia'. Defender of apostolic succession, rule of faith, incarnation, and bodily resurrection. Later attracted to Montanist rigorism c. 207; teacher of Cyprian by doctrinal influence.
Orator who presents the case against Paul before Felix in Caesarea in Acts 24.
Bishop of Antioch c. 169-183; wrote Ad Autolycum (three books) c. 180; first Christian writer to use the term Τριάς (Trinity); important witness for Logos theology.
Bishop sent by Emperor Constantius II c. 354 on a mission to Himyar (South Arabia) and possibly India; built churches in Zafar and Aden per Philostorgius HE 3.4–6; described as from Socotra island.
Member of the Twelve and witness in the Johannine resurrection narratives.
Missionary coworker of Paul and co-sender of Pauline letters.
God-fearing Corinthian whose house next to the synagogue receives Paul in Acts 18.
Pauline coworker left in Crete and addressed in the Epistle to Titus.
Pauline associate left ill at Miletus according to 2 Timothy.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 as one who labors in the Lord.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 as one who labors in the Lord.
Pauline associate sent to Ephesus according to 2 Timothy.
Figure associated with the lecture hall in Ephesus where Paul reasons daily in Acts 19.
Bishop of Rome c. 222–230; succeeded Callistus I; Hippolytus continued his rival episcopacy during Urban's pontificate.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 as a fellow worker in Christ.

Gnostic teacher; studied in Alexandria, later prominent in Rome c. 138–160. Founded Valentinianism, the most influential Gnostic school, teaching a complex pleroma of divine emanations (Aeons) and a demiurge subordinate to the supreme God. Opposed apostolic succession, bodily resurrection, and the authority of the OT.
Bishop of Rome c. 189-199; central figure in the Quartodeciman controversy; threatened to excommunicate Asian churches over Easter dating; first bishop of Rome to write in Latin.
Bishop of Rome c. 199–217; accused by Hippolytus of modalist sympathies and of being manipulated by his deacon Callistus; his pontificate saw the rise of the Logos-modalism controversy in Rome.
